coredata.dc:title: Size effect of graphene oxide from quantum dot to nanoflake on the mobility of nanoplastics in seawater-saturated sand
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Zhiqiang
Chen, Zheng
Rui, Junnan
Li, Weiying
Qiu, Yuping
coredata.dc:description: Marine sedimentary environment serves as an important sink of terrigenous nanoplastics (NP) and graphene oxides (GO). In this study, we discovered that GO of varying sizes exhibited distinct binding modes with 200 nm NP in 35 practical salinity unit (PSU) seawater, resulting in varying impacts on the mobility of NP in porous media. GO-8, with a size of 8±2 nm, firmly adhered to the surface of NP and formed stable primary heterogeneous aggregates, which promoted NP mobility and increased the mass recovery of effluent (Meff) from 24.74% to 31.08%. GO-250 (246±10 nm) partly enveloped NP and only slightly increased the volume of heteroaggregates, which had minimal effect on NP transport. Conversely, GO-850 (855±55 nm) wrapped numerous NP particles to form large secondary heteroaggregates that clung to sand surfaces, providing additional attachment sites for NP, resulting in complete inhibition of NP mobility in porous media (Meff = 0%). In brackish water with 3.5 PSU, all GO-8, GO-250 and GO-850 achieved enhanced mobility of NP, with Meff increasing from 50.35% to 85.62%, 69.45% and 75.41%, respectively. The results indicate that GO size effects on NP mobility are also salinity-dependent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoplastics
Graphene oxides
Transport
Porous media
Seawater
Brackish water

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of GO size on the transport of NP

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Tongji University
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22176148 ), Shanghai Rising-Star Program ( 23QB1406400 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Tongji University ( 2023-3-ZD-02 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Aggregation curves of different-sized GO over time (0-40 min) in brackish water (a). Aggregation curves (b) and sedimentation curves (c) of different-sized GO over time (0-40 min) in seawater. The size distribution of different-sized GO in seawater at 40 min (d).

Heteroaggregation curves of NP and different-sized GO over time (0-40 min) in brackish water (a). Heteroaggregation curves (b) and sedimentation curves (c) of NP and different-sized GO over time (0-40 min) in seawater. The size distribution of NP and different-sized GO in seawater at 40 min (d).
SEM images of NP with/without GO of varying sizes in seawater.

Breakthrough curves and retention profiles of NP with GO-8, GO-250 and GO-850 in brackish water and seawater. The retention profile of NP with GO-850 were not obtained in this study because part of heteroaggregates (agglomerates) was retained on the stainless steel screen of column in seawater.
The morphology of NP with GO-850 on sand surface in column in brackish water (a) and seawater (b).
Potential and mass balance information of colloids in column experiments under different conditions.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: MEC is the maximum effluent concentration of NPs or GO.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Meff is the mass percentages recovered from effluent.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mret is the mass percentages recovered from sand.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mtot is the total mass balances, i.e., the sum of Meff and Mret.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: h
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Individual NP or GO in binary suspension.

head.ce:title: Size effect of graphene oxide from quantum dot to nanoflake on the mobility of nanoplastics in seawater-saturated sand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering
State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resources Reuse
Shanghai Institute of Pollution Control and Ecological Security

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Environmental Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resources Reuse, Shanghai Institute of Pollution Control and Ecological Security, Tongji University, Shanghai, 200092, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Municipal Environmental Protection Engineering Co., Ltd of CERC Shanghai Group, Shanghai, 201906, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bMunicipal Environmental Protection Engineering Co., Ltd of CERC Shanghai Group, Shanghai, 201906, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China Railway Engineering Group Co., Beijing, 100039, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cChina Railway Engineering Group Co., Beijing, 100039, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Marine sedimentary environment serves as an important sink of terrigenous nanoplastics (NP) and graphene oxides (GO). In this study, we discovered that GO of varying sizes exhibited distinct binding modes with 200 nm NP in 35 practical salinity unit (PSU) seawater, resulting in varying impacts on the mobility of NP in porous media. GO-8, with a size of 8±2 nm, firmly adhered to the surface of NP and formed stable primary heterogeneous aggregates, which promoted NP mobility and increased the mass recovery of effluent (Meff) from 24.74% to 31.08%. GO-250 (246±10 nm) partly enveloped NP and only slightly increased the volume of heteroaggregates, which had minimal effect on NP transport. Conversely, GO-850 (855±55 nm) wrapped numerous NP particles to form large secondary heteroaggregates that clung to sand surfaces, providing additional attachment sites for NP, resulting in complete inhibition of NP mobility in porous media (Meff = 0%). In brackish water with 3.5 PSU, all GO-8, GO-250 and GO-850 achieved enhanced mobility of NP, with Meff increasing from 50.35% to 85.62%, 69.45% and 75.41%, respectively. The results indicate that GO size effects on NP mobility are also salinity-dependent.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoplastics (NP), which are smaller than 1 μm in size, are new emerging marine pollutants formed by the natural erosion of plastic or microplastic debris through physicochemical or biological processes. This has become a growing concern and multiple studies have shown that NP have salinity-dependent homoaggregation in seawater and size-dependent transport in seawater-saturated porous media (Hou et al., 2022; Lins et al., 2022; Venel et al., 2021). The marine sedimentary environment is the ultimate sink for NP and various non-plastic engineering nanomaterials. Therefore, the coexistence of different type of nanomaterials will inevitably affect the mobility of NP in porous media, but the underlying influence mechanism remains largely unexplored.

Hydrophilic graphene oxide (GO), an oxidized derivative of graphene, is a highly popular two-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with wide-ranging applications in new energy (Yeh et al., 2016), biomedicine(Liu et al., 2018a; Wang et al., 2022), water treatment (Dayana Priyadharshini et al., 2022), and environmental remediation (Wang et al., 2019). Nanoscale GO fragments vary in size from a few to several hundred nanometers, corresponding to the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and graphene nanoflakes (GNFs), respectively. GQDs are zero-dimensional GOs featuring a sp2-hybridized honeycomb structure with a size of less than 20 nm, and they offer high biocompatibility and antioxidant effects (Lee et al., 2020; Zheng et al., 2015). GNFs, on the other hand, are composed of one to several layers of GO sheets with diameters ranging from tens to hundreds of nanometers (Castagnola et al., 2018; Lamb et al., 2019). NP can form complex heteroaggregates with GO of various size, resulting in distinctly non-spherical shapes and even highly branched or fractal structures between inorganic and organic materials (Hochella et al., 2019; Lins et al., 2022). This poses great challenges in predicting the sedimentation and resuspension characteristics of heterogeneous aggregates at the sediment-seawater interface, leading to highly variable environmental fate for NP (Dong et al., 2019a; Hochella et al., 2019).

GO of various sizes interacts with nanoparticles in distinct ways (Alsharif et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2018b). For instance, 300 nm GO can adhere to the surface of Al2O3 particles in the presence of 0.5–20 mmol/L NaCl, while 3000 nm GO could serve as a carrier of Al2O3 particles and form gigantic heteroaggregates (Liu et al., 2018b). Feng et al. (2017) further found that GO with a hydrodynamic diameter of 300-400 nm in 50 mmol/L NaCl exhibited a higher mass percentages of GO recovered from the effluent (Meff = 93.6 %) compared to 200 nm NP (Meff = 38.0 %). This is because of the abundant oxygen-containing groups at the edge and surface defects of GO. In binary suspension, GO can attach to and carry NP, promoting NP transport in porous media. Notably, the heteroaggregation of nanoparticles may facilitate or inhibit their transport in porous media, largely depending on the size of heteroaggregates (Katzourakis and Chrysikopoulos, 2021). However, the effect of varying GO sizes on NP aggregation and GO-NP cotransport has not been extensively investigated, particularly in aquatic environments with diverse salinity gradients.

The objective of this work is to examine the aggregation and transport characteristics of polystyrene NP in seawater-saturated sea sand under the influence of GO in different sizes. This involves investigating the interaction between GO (8, 250 and 850 nm) with and NP (200 nm) in seawater with different salinity levels, analyzing the effect of GO size on the heterogeneous aggregation of GO-NP complexes, exploring the various binding modes and carrying capacities of NP with GQDs and GNFs, and proposing a GO size-dependent transport mechanism of NP in saturated porous media.

Fluorescent polystyrene nanoplastics (NP) were purchased from Shanghai Huge Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). These NP can stably suspend in aqueous solutions due to the approximately 100 μmol/g carboxyl group on their surface. A series of GO suspensions (w/w, 0.1 %) were provided by Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd., China. These suspensions were named as GO-8, GO-250 and GO-850 (Fig. S1), with hydrodynamic diameters of 8 ± 2 nm, 246 ± 10 nm and 855 ± 55 nm in deionized water, respectively. Both GO-250 and GO-850 have nanoflake structure, while GO-8 has nanodot structure.

Natural seafloor sand was collected from the seafloor of Pingtan Strait in Fujian Province of China, with details of its preparation and properties available in our previous studies (Dong et al., 2019a; Dong et al., 2019b). Sand porosity measures 44.88%, and particle size is 0.45 ± 0.03 mm. Artificial seawater with a salinity of 35 PSU was prepared by dissolving NaCl (409.4 mmol/L), Na2SO4 (28.2 mmol/L), MgCl2 (10.3 mmol/L), KCl (9.1 mmol/L) and other salts in 1000 ml deionized water(Dong et al., 2019a; Kester et al., 1967). Brackish water (3.5 PSU) was prepared by diluting artificial seawater with deionized water.

The mass concentration of the single and binary suspensions was 15 ppm for NP or GO and 15 ppm/15 ppm for NP/GO, respectively. Previous studies on the fate of NP used a similar concentration range of 4-20 ppm (Li et al., 2019a; Liu et al., 2018c; Liu et al., 2019). To achieve uniform dispersion and break up aggregates, the suspension was sonicated for 10 min using an ultrasonic cleaner (SK3200HP, KUDOS, China) to break possible aggregates and achieve uniform dispersion, followed by before aggregation and sedimentation experiments were carried out (Li et al., 2019a; Li et al., 2019b). Dynamic light scattering (Marlvern Instruments, Ltd., Worcestershire, UK) was used to measure the hydrodynamic diameter (dDLS) of particles in single and binary suspension as a function of time (0-40 min) at 25 °C in 3.5 PSU brackish water and 35 PSU seawater. These dDLS data refer to the equivalent spherical diameter, which means the same diffusion behavior as a sphere drawn around the GO sheet (Szabo et al., 2020). The intensity-weighted particle size distribution and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Zeiss Ultra 55, Germany) were used to differentiate and observe the aggregation of NP or GO and the heteroaggregation of NP and GO in both single and binary suspension at 40 min. Observations of the time-resolved optical absorbency at 330 nm using UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu Scientific Instruments, Columbia, MD) were used to examine particle sedimentation. The absorbency data were recorded every 1 min for 40 min.

A 10 cm glass column was used for the transport experiments of NP and GO since surface sediment (0-10 cm) is main sink of plastic in marine systems (Martin et al., 2017; Xue et al., 2020). The high length-diameter ratio was maintained by selecting a small inner diameter (1 cm) (Chae et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2019). The column was wet-packed with natural sea sand (11.0 ± 0.4 g, dry weight), and the stainless steel screens (pore size 50 μm) were placed at each end of the column to hold the sand and distribute water flow.

A peristaltic pump was used to inject either a suspension or background solution (brackish water or seawater) into the bottom of the column at an up-flow rate of 2.2 ± 0.1 mL/min. The column was flushed with background solution for 40 min until saturation was reached. The single and binary suspensions were then injected for 40 min at approximately 30 pore volumes (PV), followed by elution with approximately 6 PV of the same background solution. The effluent was collected in glass vials at a 2 min interval (approximately 1.5 PV). At the end of each column experiment, the sea sand in the column was carefully excavated at ∼1 cm increments and transferred to 50 mL vials containing 25 mL of deionized water. The mixtures were then shaken for 60 min to remobilize GO and NP from the sand surface, and the concentration of colloid in supernatant was calculated to determine the spatial distribution of NP and GO in column. Column experiments were conducted more than three times to ensure reproducibility of the results, and the mean data were presented. The mass balance information, which includes the mass percentages recovered from effluent, sand, and total, is shown in Table 1 . The concentrations of NP and GO in the single and binary suspension were provided in the Supplementary Material (Fig. S2). To further explore the potential cotransport mechanism of NP with GO, the transport of single NP in seawater-saturated sand column with and without GO pre-equilibration was conducted.

In brackish water, GO-8 and GO-250 was well-dispersed and their dDLS values were 8 ± 1 nm and 249 ± 18 nm, respectively. However, the dDLS of GO-850 increased from 481 ± 69 nm to 1214 ± 33 nm over 40 min (Fig. 1 a). In seawater, GO-8 remained high stable with the A/A0 of 0.97 at 40 min (Fig. 1b), while GO-250 became unstable with large aggregates (dDLS =1404 ± 35 nm at 40 min) (Fig. 1b and c). GO-850 aggregates rapidly settled from suspension with the wide size distribution ranging from 800 nm to 8000 nm (Fig. 1b), and the A/A0 value dramatically decreased to 0.48 at 40 min (Fig. 1c). These results indicate that the size-dependent aggregation of GO occurs in saline water. Smaller GO flakes show weaker aggregation compared to larger flakes.

The zeta (ζ) potential of suspension was the key factor affecting the stability of colloid (Li et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2016). Table 1 illustrates that in seawater, the ζ potentials of GO became less negative as compared to those in brackish water, thereby promoting homoaggregation of GO-250 and GO-850 (Fig. 1b). The ζ potential of GO became more negative with the increase of GO size (Table 1), suggesting that larger GO would theoretically be more stable (Jiang et al., 2016; Szabo et al., 2020). However, the GO aggregation was anomalous, which could be attributed to the flexible two-dimensional structure of GO in solution (Tang et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2016). The increase in GO size led to the transformation of 2D sheet to 1D tube-like structures with open end-caps due to interaction between cations and the GO nanoplane in a specific direction (Yang et al., 2016). Furthermore, the strong π−π interactions between GO layers facilitated the formation of 2D multi-piled structures (sheet-to-sheet aggregation) (Tang et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2016). These 1D tube-like and 2D multiple folded structures could ultimately give rise to a 3D sphere-like GO flocculation structures. Recently, Szabo et al. (2020) suggested that the aggregation, quantified by the critical coagulation concentrations (CCC), was significantly dependent on sheet size. The increase in GO size from 150 nm to 1460 nm resulted in higher van der Waals attractive forces and decreased CCC from 230 mmol/L to 50 mmol/L KCl.

GO-8, an ultra-small graphene-based nanomaterials, possesses unique physical and chemical properties due to its dual characteristics of GO and quantum dots (Kang et al., 2019; Li et al., 2017). The ionizable carboxylic acid groups at the edges of GO sheets, derived from functional groups with deprotonated edges, contribute to the stronger hydrophilicity of smaller GO with a high edge-to-area ratio (Chen and Chen, 2016; Li et al., 2017). Additionally, as a quantum dot, GO-8 exhibits a more pronounced Brownian motion in suspension which allows for more efficient dispersion compared to larger quantum dots (Szabo et al., 2020). The critical coagulation concentrations (CCC) of GO quantum dots is 2900 mmol/L NaCl, significantly higher than the salt concentration in seawater (∼500 mmol/L NaCl) (Li et al., 2017) , indicating that GO-8 can remain well-dispersed in both brackish water and seawater conditions (Fig. 1b).

The interaction between NP and GO was affected by the size of GO and the salinity of the solution. In 3.5 PSU brackish water, the dDLS of particles in binary suspension (NP and GO) increased as the size of GO increased (Fig. 2 a). The colloid size of NP with GO-8 in binary suspension was 207 ± 3 nm, similar to that of NP (202 ± 3 nm), but significantly higher than that of GO-8 (8 ± 1 nm) (Figs. 1a and 2a). The colloid size of NP with GO-850 colloids was valued 682 ± 36 nm, higher than that of NP (202 ± 3 nm), but lower than that of GO-850 (1214 ± 33 nm) in single suspension at 40 min (Figs. 1a and 2a).

Higher salinity increased colloidal heteroaggregation due to weaker electrical repulsion between NP and GO (Fig. 2b). In 35 PSU seawater, the size of NP–GO-8 heteroaggregate (216 ± 3 nm) (Fig. 2b) was slightly higher than that in brackish water (207 ± 3 nm) (Fig. 2a). The size distribution of NP–GO-8 binary suspension showed a single peak (Fig. 2c), and the peak pattern, width and position were almost the same as those of NP. SEM image further revealed that GO-8 tightly adhered to the surface of NP, making it rough (Fig. 3 a and b). These phenomena indicate that GO-8 can strongly adsorb to the NP surface and form the stable heteroaggregates, possibly due to the hydrogen bonding, Lewis acid-base and van der Waals interactions between GO-8 and NP (Liu et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2022).

In contrast, the dDLS of NP–GO-250 in binary suspension gradually increased from 248 ± 1 nm to 295 ± 12 nm over 40 min (Fig. 2b). This size range falls between that of individual NP (202 ± 2 nm) and GO-250 (1404 ± 35 nm) (Fig. 1b). Dynamic light scattering only estimates the average value of NP and GO-250 (Dong et al., 2019a; Wang et al., 2015), which is approximately equivalent to the size of individual NP. This implies that the presence of NP can alleviate the homoaggregation of GO-250 by forming NP–GO-250 heteroaggregates (Feng et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2015). The particle size distribution result indicated that NP–GO-250 had a unimodal distribution at around 250 nm (Fig. 2c). In comparison, GO-250 homoaggregates exhibited a peak at approximately 1000 nm but completely dispersed in binary suspension (Figs. 2c and 1c). Moreover, the SEM image showed that GO-250 was wrapped around NP and covered the NP surface. The volume of NP–GO-250 heteroaggregates remains nearly unchanged (Fig. 4 c).

However, in binary suspension where the size of GO was 850 nm, aggregation was dominated by GO-850. The particle size and A/A0 of binary suspension attained values of 3373 ± 290 nm and 0.49 after 40 min, respectively (Fig. 2b and d), which is comparable to the data of GO-850 in single suspension (Fig. 1d and b). In a binary NP–GO-250 suspension, the particle size distribution exhibited bimodal characteristics (Figs. 1c and 2c), which differs from a unimodal distribution observed with NP (∼200 nm) and GO-850 (∼3000 nm) in the single suspension. The peak around 350 nm might be produced by a single GO-850 coated with a single NP particle, and the peak around 2000 nm might be from GO-850 homoaggregates or a large volume of NP-GO heteroaggregates. As per the SEM images, numerous NP were anchored to the GO-850 flakes, resulting in forming bulky heteroaggregate (Fig. 3d). Feng et al. (2017) observed that the two-dimensional GO (500-1000 nm) provided attachment sites for 100 nm hematite nanoparticles, leading to the formation of GO-hematite heteroaggregates, similar to our findings.

Table 1 shows that the total mass balance (Mtot) of GO-8 and GO-250 in seawater-saturated sand was 98.92 % and 107.02 %, respectively. Fig. S3a and c indicate that GO-8 and GO-250 were detected in the column effluent after approximately one PV, suggesting that size exclusion does not play a significant role in GO transport (Babakhani et al., 2017; Kasel et al., 2013). Rather, GO size and aquatic conditions determine its transport and retention in the column.

In brackish water, all GO types were highly mobile (Fig. S3a), with maximum effluent concentrations (MEC) of 1.00, 0.95 and 0.91for GO-8, GO-250 and GO-850, respectively (Table 1). However, unlike the plateau breakthrough curve (BTC) of GO-8, the BTC of GO-250 and GO-850 exhibited a gradual ascent towards the peak (Fig. S3a), indicating site-blocking phenomena. This refers to a decrease in the deposition rate as the number of attached particles increases (Babakhani et al., 2017). Such an effect is due to the growing occupancy of the attachment site by colloidal particles on the sand surface (Dong et al., 2018). Previous studies have shown that the blocking effect dominated the transport of GO in porous media, with strong repulsion between GO and GO attached to sand particles (Beryani et al., 2020; He et al., 2015).

There was minimal difference in the mass percentages recovered from the effluent (Meff) of GO-8 in seawater and brackish water (98.92% and 99.88%, respectively) (Table 1). This similarity can be attributed to the insensitivity of the change in ζ potential and dDLS of GO-8 with regards to salinity (Table 1, Fig. 1a and b). However, GO-250 and GO-850 showed a sharp drop in Meff from 74.10 % and 79.93 % to 41.83 % and 0 %, respectively, as salinity increased from 3.5 PSU to 35 PSU (Table 1). This was due to increased aggregation of GO and reduced electrostatic repulsion between GO and sand, as salinity increases (Peng et al., 2017; Xia et al., 2015). The strong aggregation and rapid deposition of GO-850 completely inhibited its mobility and enhanced its retention in the column (Fig. 1b, d and Fig. S3a). Folded GO-850 aggregates on the sand surface further indicated their difficult preservation through sand channels (Fig. S4). The experimental results are in general agreement with the previous theoretical findings that the initial particle diameter distribution of migrating particles is expected to affect the average size of aggregates and in turn influence their mobility in a porous media (Katzourakis and Chrysikopoulos, 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of GO size on the transport of NP
ce:section.ce:para: In sand column saturated with brackish water, all GOs were found to enhance the transport of NP and inhibit their retention (Fig. 4a and b). In a binary suspension, the Meff of NP increased from 50.35 % to 85.62 %, 69.45 % and 75.41 % upon exposure to GO-8, GO-250 and GO-850, respectively (Table 1). The MEC of NP with GO-8, GO-250 and GO-850 were also significantly higher than that (0.58) observed in single suspension (Table 1) with values of 0.98, 0.79 and 0.85, respectively. Interestingly, the effect of GO on NP transport was not proportional to their size, and the rank order of influence was GO-8 > GO-850 > GO-250. This is due to the fact that at the same mass concentration, a large number of GO-8 can tightly bind to NP particles, significantly enhancing NP transport. Although used in a small amount, GO-850 can also completely encapsulate NP and promote NP transport due to its largest size. However, GO-250, being of medium quantity and size, can only cover a portion of the NP surface, resulting in the weakest facilitation of NP transport.

In a seawater-saturated sand column, GO-8 was found to accelerate NP mobility, increasing Meff from 24.74 % to 31.08 % (Table 1). The ζ potential of NP–GO-8 suspension in seawater was -9.94 ± 0.06 mV, which was higher (less negative) than that of NP (-25.20 ± 1.05 mV) in single suspension (Table 1). Theoretical reduction of surface charge implied a decrease in electrostatic repulsion between NP and sand grains with higher chances of NP retention, but this was not observed (Fig. 4c and d) (Georgopoulou et al., 2020; Li et al., 2019a). Therefore, GO-induced changes in electrostatic interaction were not found to be the main factor affecting NP migration in seawater.

Competition for deposition sites on sand surfaces by coexisting substances may potentially contribute to the transport of colloids in columns, according to studies by He et al. (2019) and Li et al. (2019a). In this study, we further pre-equilibrated the sand column with GO-8 under 35 PSU seawater conditions and then injected single NP suspension into the column to obtain BTC. If there is a competition for deposition sites between GO-8 and NP, GO-8 will preferentially occupy the deposition sites on the surface of the sand grains, which enhances the NP mobility. However, the results showed that GO-8 pre-equilibration had little effect on NP transport (Fig. S5). This demonstrated that enhanced transport of NP in binary suspension was not due to deposition competition of GO-8.

Previous studies indicated that coexisting particles with higher transportability in suspensions can act as mobile carriers for particles with lower transportability, thereby facilitating the latter's mobility in porous media (Cai et al., 2013; Dong et al., 2019a; He et al., 2021). The adsorption of GO-8 on NP was similar to the “corona” formed by organic matter on the surface of nanoparticles (Fig. 3b). This could alter their surface properties and transport behavior, thereby facilitating particle transport (Dong et al., 2020; Galloway et al., 2017).

GO-250 had negligible effect on NP transport and the Meff of NP–GO-250 (26.82%) was slightly higher than that of NP (24.74 %) in single suspension under 35 PSU seawater condition (Table 1). The dDLS of NP–GO-250 measured 295 ± 12 nm over 40 min, which was larger than that of NP–GO-8 (216 ± 3 nm) (Fig. 2b). Larger heteroaggregates resulted in a stronger physical straining effect and the lower mobility (Babakhani et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2021). Additionally, GO-250 had lower mobility at 41.83 % compared to GO-8 at 98.92 % in sand columns saturated with seawater (Table 1 and Fig. S3c), resulting in a low NP-carrying capacity of GO-250.

However, GO-850 completely inhibited the mobility of NP with Meff = 0 in binary suspension (Fig. 4c, Table 1). GO-850 enveloped the NP like a giant net (Fig. 5 b), forming gigantic secondary heteroaggregates of NP–GO-850 in 35 PSU seawater (Fig. 3d). The GO-850 coverage on sand surface altered the heterogeneity of sand surfaces, creating more deposition sites for NP attachment through the bridging effect, ultimately lowering colloidal transport. When the sand was pre-covered with GO-850, the transport of NP was lower compared to the bare sand column (Fig. S5), indicating that the additional retention sites provided by GO-850 played a vital role in minimizing NP transport. Moreover, NP–GO-850 would be physically strained by its entrapment in small sand pores and could even formed the multi-layer deposition (i.e., ripening) on the sand surface, which could clog the pores and reduce elution over time (Alimi et al., 2021; Babakhani et al., 2017).

The salinity gradient in estuary system affects the scale effect of GO on the mobility of NP in the column. In brackish water, all three scales of GO can promote NP mobility, but this facilitation is limited by increasing salinity, which can even reduce NP transport. The compressed double electric layer of particle-sand and particle-particle is a significant factor in this phenomenon with higher salinity (Li et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2021). The ζ potential of NP with GO-8, GO-250 and GO-850 in seawater is -9.94 ± 0.06 mV, -13.45 ± 1.65 mV and -15.35 ± 0.05 mV, respectively, which are higher (more positive) than those (-28.65 ± 1.15 mV, -28.90 ± 0.10 mV and -34.30 ± 0.40 mV) in brackish water (Table 1). The weaker electrostatic repulsion between particles lead to stronger heteroaggregation and larger heteroaggregates in seawater (Fig. 2a and b), triggering higher straining and gravity effect (Babakhani et al., 2017; Chrysikopoulos and Syngouna, 2014). This ultimately increases NP retention (Fig. 4a-d) and inhibits the transport of GO-8 and GO-250 from brackish water to seawater (Fig. S6a and c, Fig. S7a and c). The effect of salinity variation on GO-850 is more pronounced. Under brackish water conditions, GO-850 only encapsulates a single NP (Fig. 5a and S8), while in seawater conditions, GO-850 can act as a bridge between multiple NP, enhancing their attachment, as observed by SEM (Figs. 3d and 5b). Similarly, increasing salinity reduces the electrical charge of sand, weakening electrostatic repulsion between particle and sand, which inhibits NP transport in a binary suspension (Dong et al., 2019a; Peng et al., 2017; Venel et al., 2021).

The widespread application and environmental release of GO have made it possible to interact with NP in marine and estuarine environments. It is crucial to understand the effect of GO on the transport of NP to predict their environmental fates accurately. In this study, we used GO of different size (8 nm, 250 nm and 850 nm) to investigate their influence on the transport of NP in sea sand saturated with 3.5 PSU brackish water and 35 PSU seawater. Our results highlight the significant impact of GO size on NP mobility under different salinity gradients. In brackish water, all sizes of GO significantly enhanced the mobility of NP in natural sea sand. However, in seawater, GO of different size showed varying effects on NP transport. The promotion of GO for NP mobility decreased with an increase in GO size from 8 nm, 250 nm, to 850 nm due to the larger size and lower stability of the NP–GO heteroaggregates. These findings confirm salinity-dependent effect of GO size on NP transport. The above knowledge will help to deeply understand the interaction between GO and NP in marine and estuarine environments, and to assess the risk of joint environmental exposure of GO with NP.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22176148), Shanghai Rising-Star Program (23QB1406400), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Tongji University (2023-3-ZD-02).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.watres.2023.120491.
